posts.content:
Python is one of my favourite language. It is very intuitive, simple and a powerful language. It’s power comes from its ability to give the developers the freedom to manipulate the language at run time. One such ability is how a programmer can intercept the control during class object creation and change its outlook entirely from what programmer intented. This is achieved through a language feature called metaclass.
metaclass is a class of a class. We know that in python everything is an object. Which means there is a class or blueprint for every object we see. In python class is also an object and the thing that creates it is called the metaclass. In python it is the type class. It is a built in entity and there is no way to recreate it and since it is a class, we can inherit it. This we will discuss in the coming sections.
Let’s analyze the code a little bit to see what we discussed is correct:
>>> class Test: ... pass ... >>> t = Test() >>> t.__class__ # t is instance of class Test. <class '__main__.Test'> >>> Test.__class__ # Test is an instance of type class. <class 'type'>  We can also instantiate class objects at runtime. Although it looks bit contrived but we can achieve it. The method signature to do that is: type(class_name, base_classes_tupe, dict).
>>> def init(self, name, age): ... self.name = name ... self.age = age ... >>> Person = type("Person", (object,), {"__init__": init}) >>> x = Person("hello world", 100) >>> x <__main__.Person object at 0x10c80c210> >>> x.name 'hello world' >>> x.age 100 >>> y = Person("hello world", 200) >>> y.age 200  We saw that we can create classes at runtime using type. But can we manipulate the class definition when it is defined in the traditional/usual way ? yes we can and this is where metaclass and magic methods come into play.
metaclass and __new__, __init__, __call__ magic methods: We know that a metaclass is a class which creates classes. In python the default metaclass for all classes is type. And we saw how it was used to instantiate class objects. So if we as a developer want to control or manipulate class object creation, we have to come up with our own metaclass. Since type is a class, we can do that by inherting it.
Also before we begin, the magic methods __new__, __init__, __call__ are nothing to do with metaclass. You can define them on any class and it does not have to inherit type class. They all deal with objects but in case of metaclass, these objects are classes. We just need to keep that in mind. Also these executed in the following order: __new__, __init__, __call__.
Without further ado, lets create our first metaclass. In this example, we will simply print the arguments these magic methods receive. This will help us understand when these methods are called. After that we will try to implement singleton, factory pattern and adding getter and setter by default to class attributes.
class DefaultMeta(type): def __new__(mcs, name, bases, attrs): # mcs is the metaclass: DefaultMeta # name: class name it is about to create. # bases: parent classes defined on the class. # attrs: attributes defined in the class: this includes class properties and methods. Attributes added in the classes __init__ method(which deals with objects of class) are not received here. print("mcs:", mcs) print("name:", name) print("bases:", bases) print("attrs:", attrs) return super().__new__(mcs, name, bases, attrs) # here mcs needs to be explicitly passed as first argument def __init__(cls, name, bases, attrs): # same arguments except for the first argument. Here the class object is already created. print("cls:", cls) print("name:", name) print("bases:", bases) print("attrs:", attrs) super().__init__(name, bases, attrs) # __init__ here is an instance method so no need to pass `cls` in the arguments. def __call__(cls, *args, **kwargs): # called when user tries to instantiate an object: ClassName(*args, **kwargs) print("args:", args) print("kwargs:", kwargs) obj = super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) return obj class Test(metaclass=DefaultMeta): some_prop = 100 def test(): print("hello world") mcs: <class '__main__.DefaultMeta'> name: Test bases: () attrs: {'__module__': '__main__', '__qualname__': 'Test', 'some_prop': 100, 'test': <function Test.test at 0x10c7f4dd0>} cls: <class '__main__.Test'> name: Test bases: () attrs: {'__module__': '__main__', '__qualname__': 'Test', 'some_prop': 100, 'test': <function Test.test at 0x10c7f4dd0>}  So you can see that as soon as interpreter encountered the class definition, it invoked our metaclass to create it. The steps could go something like this:
Test = DefaultMeta.__new__(DefaultMeta, "Test", (object,), {"some_prop": 100, "test": func_ref}) DefaultMeta.__init__(Test, "Test", (object,), {"some_prop": 100, "test": func_ref})  __call__ will be called when you try to create an object of Test.
Just for fun what we can do is, create a RogueMeta class which simply wipes all the attributes from the class. Though it is a useless but we can get gist of what we can achieve with the metaclasses.
class RogueMeta(type): def __new__(mcs, name, bases, attrs): return super().__new__(mcs, name, (), {}) class Test(metaclass=RogueMeta): some_prop = 100 def __init__(self, x): self.x = x def test(): print("hello world") z = Test(100) print(z.x)  you should get this error when you execute the above code block:
Traceback (most recent call last): File "./test.py", line 16, in <module> z = Test(100) TypeError: Test() takes no arguments  Remember we can’t achieve this in __init__ method because class is already created by that time. So we need to intercept just before the class object is created. Otherwise in most of the cases all heavy lifting can be done inside __init__ method.
Singleton pattern using metaclass: Singleton pattern, we want to restric the instance of a class to one single instance. Mutiple call to the creation of objects should return already created object. So we need to intercept the object creation step which is __call__ method of the metaclass. Remember __new__ and __init__ are called when interpreter encounters the class definition and it happens only one time. __call__ is called whenever we want to create an object of the class which is what we need in this scenario.
class Singleton(type): _cache = {} def __call__(cls, *args, **kwargs): if cls not in cls._cache: cls._cache[cls] = super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) return cls._cache[cls] class Test(metaclass=Singleton): pass a = Test() b = Test() print(a) print(b) # both should print the same address location.
posts.summary:
Python is one of my favourite language. It is very intuitive, simple and a powerful language. It’s power comes from its ability to give the developers the freedom to manipulate the language at run time. One such ability is how a programmer can intercept the control during class object creation and change its outlook entirely from what programmer intented. This is achieved through a language feature called metaclass.
metaclass is a class of a class.
posts.title: Python Metaclass and It's Magic Methods

posts.content:
I always wanted to blog as far as I can remember but for some reason it never came to fruition. Probably because I am very lazy. But this time I have made my mind that I will blog every week no matter how silly the posts are. This gives me an opportunity to introspect about my life and also a nice way to improve my english language skills.
I don’t have to mention how good 2020 is for everyone :). I love travelling and I wanted to visit so many coutries this year but all that dream went up in smoke thanks to covid :(. I was lucky to visit Switzerland before the pandemic reared its ugly face. It was a fantastic trip, seeing snow caped mountains and sipping hot chocolate is the best feeling in the world. I hope someone will find a cure for covid as soon as possible so that we can all start living our life just like before.
On learning front, I wanted to concentrate more on system design, kubernetes, postgresql, react and understanding the concepts of distributed systems. I can’t say that I have achieved everything but now I know more about kubernetes and postgresql than ever before. I will write more about k8s and postgresql in the coming weeks.
I can’t recommend this course enough if you want to improve your system design skills. Though this is more oriented towards acing the interviews but for a beginner this is a good starting point. It will cover enough ground and you will also know where to look for if you want to learn more in the future.
Also Microsoft Azure Architectures Center is worth reading if you want to dig deeper into system design.
By the end of this year I would like to complete following tasks:
 Complete reading Data Intensive Applications Complete React course from React, Redux Complete Redis In Action Complete Kafka Definitive Guide Complete Disitribute System Course on Courseera  Bye.
posts.summary:
I always wanted to blog as far as I can remember but for some reason it never came to fruition. Probably because I am very lazy. But this time I have made my mind that I will blog every week no matter how silly the posts are. This gives me an opportunity to introspect about my life and also a nice way to improve my english language skills.
I don’t have to mention how good 2020 is for everyone :).
posts.title: 2020 3/4 In Review
